DESN2003 Doc 0.1 - Independent Cohort Assessment

Assessment Date: 2026-03-17
Assessor: Independent AI review for instructor validation
Grading Basis: 50% effort + 50% Doc 0.1 quality, then curved to median 85, min 75, max 96

Assessment Basis

This assessment was built from the course-side Doc 0.1 expectations in assessment.qmd and data/feedback_principles.txt, not from the existing D01 assessment writeup. The quality half evaluates:

  • thematic synthesis rather than source-by-source summary
  • relevance and credibility of sources
  • critical evaluation of limitations, methods, or blind spots
  • whether the review clearly builds toward a research gap

The effort half evaluates:

  • breadth of reading and evidence of actual literature searching
  • completeness and development of the review
  • structural care, citation preparation, and overall polish

Curve method: piecewise-linear mapping from the raw cohort distribution to the requested anchors.
Raw min 13 -> 75, raw median 67 -> 85, raw max 89 -> 96.

Cohort Summary

This cohort is split into three fairly clear bands. The top band already understands that a literature review is an argument about a field rather than a list of sources. Those submissions organize the reading into themes, compare traditions or methods, and arrive at a gap that feels earned. The middle band usually has a workable topic and some relevant reading, but the writing still slips back into summary, the critical lens is inconsistent, or the evidence base is thinner than it needs to be. The lower band is mainly a completeness problem: too few sources, too little writing, or a document that is closer to a proposal than to a review.

Common strengths - Most students have a real topic rather than a vague area of interest. - Several students are already trying to identify methodological or conceptual blind spots, not just reporting findings. - Many reviews have the beginnings of a usable Doc 1 direction even when the current draft is underdeveloped.

Common weaknesses - Summary still dominates synthesis in much of the cohort. - Source bases are often too small for a literature review; several submissions sit closer to 2-6 sources than the expected 8-12. - Critical evaluation is the most common missing move: many papers report findings but do not ask what the cited work could not answer. - Gap sections are often asserted rather than demonstrated.

Grade Table

Rank Student Quality Effort Raw Curved
1 Li Ping Sum 43 46 89 96
2 Yu Qiheng 43 42 85 94
3 Ho Chun 41 39 80 92
4 Wong Chun Tin Isaac 41 38 79 91
5 Yang Zhen 39 39 78 90
6 Bai Junyi 36 35 71 87
7 Wong Ho Yan 34 34 68 86
8 Yu Yunqi 33 34 67 85
9 Zhuang Victor 31 32 63 84
10 Chan Yuen Yau Davina 29 33 62 84
11 Chan Kiu Chin 31 25 56 83
12 Pradhan Arjun Kumar 29 24 53 82
13 Patwa Neil Amit 24 22 46 81
14 Mahajan Ashalle Kumar 22 19 41 80
15 Ye Lo A (Lucile) 7 6 13 75

Curve stats: Median = 85, Min = 75, Max = 96

Individual Evaluations

1. Li Ping Sum - Curved 96

Topic: FOMO and nostalgia-based emotional branding strategies driving emotional consumption across Generation Z
Quality: 43/50 | Effort: 46/50 | Raw: 89

What worked - This is one of the few submissions that really behaves like a literature review: it moves through concepts, mechanisms, and market strategies in a deliberate sequence. - The reading effort is substantial. There is clear evidence of broad searching across academic, industry, and applied sources. - The gap is more than a generic “more research is needed” claim; it identifies a reciprocal loop between Gen Z traits and branding strategy.

What needs work - Some of the definitional and trend sources are too lightweight for core conceptual claims, and at least a few references look mismatched or shaky. - The brand-strategy section becomes more descriptive than critical. It explains what brands are doing better than it evaluates the evidence behind those claims.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Audit the references carefully, replace the weakest definitional sources, and add a more explicit evaluative lens to the brand-strategy section.

2. Yu Qiheng - Curved 94

Topic: The social dynamics of sustainable behavior change
Quality: 43/50 | Effort: 42/50 | Raw: 85

What worked - The structure is excellent. The review stages three literature conversations and shows exactly how each helps and where each runs out. - The writing is controlled and synthetic. The gap around the “interpersonal middle ground” feels earned by the review rather than attached at the end. - The source base is strong and academically credible.

What needs work - The review is more conceptually critical than methodologically critical; it evaluates streams of work but spends less time interrogating particular studies. - The bibliography leans heavily on foundational sources, so the conversation can feel slightly historically anchored rather than current.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Add a small cluster of post-2020 sources and one paragraph that directly critiques methods, not just theories.

3. Ho Chun - Curved 92

Topic: Architectural elements in FPS game maps and player positioning/playstyle
Quality: 41/50 | Effort: 39/50 | Raw: 80

What worked - This is one of the strongest analytic reviews in the set. It consistently asks what each source can explain and what it still leaves open. - The bridge from urban or architectural theory into game-space analysis is thoughtful rather than superficial. - The gap is specific and researchable: measurable links between map architecture and player behavior remain underdeveloped.

What needs work - The file still contains a large amount of Doc 0 material, so the submission is doing two jobs at once. - Several important sources are foundational rather than recent, and the empirical base could be expanded.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Strip out the Doc 0 framing, expand the recent empirical game-studies reading, and keep the same critical tone.

4. Wong Chun Tin Isaac - Curved 91

Topic: Platform-mediated waste generation in Gen Z collecting practices
Quality: 41/50 | Effort: 38/50 | Raw: 79

What worked - The review is well designed. It separates platform-mediated consumption, identity/display logics, and environmental consequences, then reconnects them through the gap. - The sources are well chosen and mostly credible. - The gap is strong because it emerges from the fact that the three literatures are not actually speaking to one another.

What needs work - A few key sources do a lot of the work across multiple sections, which limits the breadth a bit. - There are enough grammar and phrasing errors to blunt the polish of an otherwise strong review.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Add more collecting-specific or fandom-specific literature and tighten the proofreading.

5. Yang Zhen - Curved 90

Topic: Tangible and intangible dimensions of bookstore user experience
Quality: 39/50 | Effort: 39/50 | Raw: 78

What worked - The tangible/intangible structure gives the review a clear internal logic and helps it avoid becoming a list of papers. - The submission shows real effort to connect design, service, and bookstore experience rather than treating them as separate problems. - The final justification for a Hong Kong-specific study is directionally persuasive.

What needs work - Some evidence is only indirectly about bookstores, so the transfer from library or general retail research sometimes feels asserted rather than proven. - The Hong Kong section starts to read more like project rationale than literature synthesis.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Bring in more bookstore-specific and Hong Kong-specific sources, then tighten the synthesis in the intangible/service sections.

6. Bai Junyi - Curved 87

Topic: “Ugly aesthetics” in blind box character design and university students’ consumption behavior
Quality: 36/50 | Effort: 35/50 | Raw: 71

What worked - The review has a clean conceptual arc from aesthetic shift, to blind-box market expression, to student-specific consumption. - The gap is clear and reasonably specific: the psychological mechanism behind student attraction to ugly-aesthetic blind boxes still needs explanation. - The topic is distinctive and potentially strong for later design research.

What needs work - The evidence base is still thin for a literature review. A noticeable share of the support comes from market or web-facing sources rather than a deeper academic cluster. - Theoretical references are introduced but not critically worked very hard.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Expand the peer-reviewed reading, especially around symbolic consumption and collectible psychology, and spend more time evaluating the explanatory power of each theory.

7. Wong Ho Yan - Curved 86

Topic: Design features of pop culture collectibles and emotional consumption in Hong Kong among Gen Z
Quality: 34/50 | Effort: 34/50 | Raw: 68

What worked - The review is trying to do more than summarize. It makes a real attempt to identify a methodological overreliance on questionnaires. - The distinction between blind boxes and the wider TTC category is a useful narrowing move. - There is a workable gap around the post-purchase and physical-life dimensions of collectibles.

What needs work - Several sources feel lightweight or peripheral, which weakens the authority of the review. - Some claims about neglected dimensions are plausible, but the supporting literature base is not broad enough yet.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Anchor the paper in stronger consumer-research, material-culture, or emotional-design scholarship, then keep the current focus on physical engagement.

8. Yu Yunqi - Curved 85

Topic: Ski tourism destination choice and digital recommendation systems
Quality: 33/50 | Effort: 34/50 | Raw: 67

What worked - The review has a smart applied structure: it places academic decision-making research beside actual platform behavior. - The platform critique is useful and specific, especially around how current tools flatten ski-specific needs. - The knowledge-based recommender direction is a sensible gap.

What needs work - The academic base is not deep enough yet, and too much weight falls on a few studies plus website descriptions. - The review critiques platforms more strongly than it critiques the literature itself.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Expand the academic side with recommender-systems and travel-UX literature so the platform critique rests on a firmer knowledge base.

9. Zhuang Victor - Curved 84

Topic: Preservation of Jiaohe Ancient City
Quality: 31/50 | Effort: 32/50 | Raw: 63

What worked - The topic is specific, serious, and well worth researching. - The references are relevant to the technical problem and show targeted searching rather than random collection. - The review does identify a practical tension between preservation and intervention.

What needs work - The review is too narrow and too short to map the wider preservation landscape with confidence. - The gap is there, but it is still written more as a continuation of the topic than as a sharply stated research contribution.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Broaden the review beyond anchoring techniques and rewrite the gap as a fuller statement of what is missing, why it matters, and what your study will actually contribute.

10. Chan Yuen Yau Davina - Curved 84

Topic: Passive design strategies, natural ventilation, and AI integration in building planning
Quality: 29/50 | Effort: 33/50 | Raw: 62

What worked - There is obvious effort here: the source list is broader than many in the cohort, and the Hong Kong context is kept visible. - The review is trying to connect traditional passive design knowledge with newer AI-supported modeling approaches. - The practical orientation is clear.

What needs work - The writing is still largely source-by-source summary. The literature is being reported, not really synthesized. - The gap section is the weakest part. It states a belief in the value of AI integration, but it does not yet precisely identify what the literature has failed to establish.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Rebuild each paragraph around a theme rather than a single source, and rewrite the gap in concrete terms: what has already been modeled, what has not, and what your study will test or compare.

11. Chan Kiu Chin - Curved 83

Topic: Vacuum-based furniture installation on rough domestic surfaces
Quality: 31/50 | Effort: 25/50 | Raw: 56

What worked - The topic is tightly framed and the literature chosen is technically relevant. - The review identifies a genuine engineering tension between conformity and long-term creep. - The final gap is specific and testable.

What needs work - This is much too short for the assignment. Right now it reads more like a compressed outline of a review than a developed one. - The source range is too narrow, and the missing reference list hurts completeness and polish.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Keep the current problem framing, but expand the evidence base substantially and add a full reference list.

12. Pradhan Arjun Kumar - Curved 82

Topic: Gacha pity systems and limited-time rewards in mobile gaming
Quality: 29/50 | Effort: 24/50 | Raw: 53

What worked - The topic is focused and the core tension in the literature is visible. - The cited work is relevant to the question and there is at least some attempt to put sources into conversation. - The paper is moving toward a researchable question rather than staying at a broad topic level.

What needs work - Five sources is still too thin for a literature review on a topic with obvious adjacent literatures. - The gap section is underdeveloped and ends too quickly once the disagreement in the literature is named.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Add adjacent work on gambling psychology, FOMO, monetization design, and regulation, then turn the gap into a full paragraph rather than a short note.

13. Patwa Neil Amit - Curved 81

Topic: Fitness tracking applications and long-term engagement
Quality: 24/50 | Effort: 22/50 | Raw: 46

What worked - The writing has a readable voice and a clear concern with user experience rather than just product features. - There is a visible conceptual contrast between self-tracking discipline and data overload. - The disengaged-user angle could become a useful research direction.

What needs work - Two sources is nowhere near enough for a literature review. - The current document is too short and too light on actual literature to support the gap with confidence.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Build a real review around health-app adoption, behavior-change theory, gamification, and retention research before refining the product direction.

14. Mahajan Ashalle Kumar - Curved 80

Topic: Reddit’s anecdotal knowledge versus fact-centric websites
Quality: 22/50 | Effort: 19/50 | Raw: 41

What worked - The underlying question is timely and genuinely interesting. - The problem framing is understandable and the contrast between anecdotal and authoritative knowledge is clear. - There is a real gap claim here, not just a topic statement.

What needs work - This document is structured more like a research proposal than a literature review. - The source base is too small and the reference handling is incomplete, so the review never becomes a real synthesis of a field.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Rewrite the piece as a literature review with themed sections on platform trust, online advice communities, experiential knowledge, and perceived helpfulness.

15. Ye Lo A (Lucile) - Curved 75

Topic: Social anxiety and appearance anxiety
Quality: 7/50 | Effort: 6/50 | Raw: 13

What worked - The topic itself has clear design-for-wellbeing potential. - The few studies named do appear directly relevant to the question.

What needs work - The submission is far too short to count as a literature review. - It summarizes a few findings but does not synthesize, evaluate, or identify a developed gap.

Best next move for Doc 1 - Treat this as a reset: build a proper thematic review with 8-12 sources, then write a clear gap statement from that base.

Validation Against assessment_doc01.md

This independent review broadly corroborates the existing assessment already in the folder.

Strong agreement - The top band is essentially the same: Li Ping Sum, Yu Qiheng, Ho Chun, Wong Chun Tin Isaac, and Yang Zhen remain the strongest group. - The lower band is also stable: Ye Lo A, Mahajan Ashalle Kumar, Patwa Neil Amit, and Pradhan Arjun Kumar all remain near the bottom because of completeness and evidence-base problems rather than topic weakness. - 12/15 curved grades land within 1 point of the existing assessment, and all 15/15 land within 2 points.

Main differences - Bai Junyi is scored a bit lower here because the evidence base feels less mature than the current D01 assessment suggests; the conceptual direction is good, but the review still needs more academic depth. - Chan Yuen Yau Davina is scored slightly higher on effort here because the breadth of gathering is visible even though the synthesis is still weak. - Ho Chun and Yu Qiheng both score slightly higher here because the structural and analytic quality of the review writing itself is especially strong.

Specific validation note - The earlier possible overlap flag between Ho Chun and Chan Yuen Yau Davina does not hold up on independent reading. The documents are clearly on different topics and use different literatures. The most likely cause is noisy automated similarity detection from the combined Doc 0 + Doc 0.1 format in Davina’s file.

Bottom line - The existing assessment is directionally reliable. The only submission I would most want rechecked manually if fine-grained ranking matters is Bai Junyi, because that is where my independent read diverged the most.

These grades are tentative and should be treated as instructor-facing working grades, not final released marks. They are suitable for moderation and later curving decisions, not as the last human judgment.

 

DESN2003: Research for Innovation · University of Hong Kong
